title: D.B. Cooper: The Man Who Vanished From the Sky

segments.text: Some criminals get caught. Some quietly disappear.
But only one man committed the perfect crime in mid-air.
He jumped out of a passenger jet with a fortune, and was never seen again.
No body. No name. No answers. For over fifty years.
This is the story of D.B. Cooper.
It began on a cold November evening in 1971.
At the airport in Portland, Oregon, a man in a dark suit bought a one-way ticket to Seattle.
He paid in cash, and gave his name as Dan Cooper.
A reporting error would later turn that name into D.B. Cooper.
He boarded the Boeing 727 calmly and took a seat near the back.
He ordered a drink, lit a cigarette, and waited.
Then he handed a flight attendant a note.
At first she ignored it, thinking it was just a man's number.
But he leaned in and said, quietly, I have a bomb.
He opened his briefcase just enough to reveal wires and red cylinders.
His demands were simple, and chillingly precise.
Two hundred thousand dollars in cash. And four parachutes.
The crew radioed ahead, and authorities decided to cooperate.
The plane circled above Seattle while the money was gathered.
When it landed, the ransom and parachutes were brought on board.
True to his word, Cooper released all the passengers.
But he kept several crew members on the plane.
Then he gave a new order. Fly toward Mexico City.
Slow, and low, with the landing gear down.
Somewhere over the dark forests of Washington, he made his move.
He lowered the rear stairway of the plane, into the freezing night air.
And then, at around eight in the evening, he jumped.
Into the rain, the wind, and the darkness below.
When the plane finally landed, the man was gone.
He left behind only a clip-on tie and a few cigarette butts.
And so began the largest manhunt the region had ever seen.
Agents combed the forests for any trace of him.
They found nothing. No parachute, no body, no money.
For years, the case went completely cold.
Then, in 1980, a boy made a discovery on a riverbank.
Buried in the sand were rotting bundles of twenty dollar bills.
The serial numbers matched Cooper's ransom exactly.
It was the only part of the money ever found.
By now, his legend had grown far beyond the crime itself.
It even reached pop culture. In the show Prison Break, a character named Charles Westmoreland claims to be D.B. Cooper himself.
Over the decades, the FBI chased hundreds of real suspects.
Not one was ever confirmed to be the man on that plane.
In 2016, after forty five years, the FBI finally closed the case.
Did Cooper survive that jump into the storm? Or did the forest swallow him forever?
Some criminals get caught. Some quietly disappear.
But D.B. Cooper stepped into the clouds, and into legend.
The only skyjacker who was never caught, and never found.
This was just one case the world never forgot. Subscribe. There are many more.

description: The full story of D.B. Cooper, the only unsolved skyjacking in American history.
